refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve and maintain operational availability of ≥ 0.97 (97%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as (total scheduled operating time − unplanned downtime) / total scheduled operating time, accounting for all failure modes across vehicle, teleoperation, and safety subsystems.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-004 is a system-level KPI that directly impacts customer value and mission success. Refinement clarifies the measurement methodology, evaluation period, and scope of downtime contributors. High safety criticality requires explicit inclusion of safety-induced unavailability in the availability calculation.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Continuous telemetry logging of operational state transitions (ready, operating, degraded, failed, maintenance); automated availability calculation dashboard; post-evaluation audit against agreed baseline and customer acceptance criteria.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: Vehicle subsystem shall contribute ≤ 0.015 (1.5%) unplanned downtime to total availability budget.
FR-NGHE-004.2: Teleoperation subsystem shall contribute ≤ 0.010 (1.0%) unplanned downtime to total availability budget.
FR-NGHE-004.3: Safety Services subsystem shall contribute ≤ 0.005 (0.5%) unplanned downtime to total availability budget.
FR-NGHE-004.4: System shall detect and log all downtime events with root cause classification within 5 minutes of occurrence.
FR-NGHE-004.5: System shall provide predictive maintenance recommendations to prevent unplanned failures.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement redundancy and fault tolerance across all critical subsystems (vehicle drivetrain, teleoperation network, safety sensors) with automatic failover and graceful degradation.
DP-B: Establish preventive maintenance schedule based on usage hours and predictive health monitoring; maintain spare parts inventory and rapid response maintenance team.
DP-C: Design modular architecture with hot-swappable components (battery packs, communication modules, sensor arrays) to minimize mean time to repair (MTTR).
DP-D: Implement comprehensive health monitoring and telemetry system that continuously tracks subsystem performance, predicts failures, and triggers maintenance alerts before failure occurs.
DP-E: Combine DP-A (redundancy), DP-C (modularity), and DP-D (health monitoring) into an integrated availability assurance strategy.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-E: Integrated availability assurance combining redundancy/fault tolerance (DP-A), modular hot-swappable architecture (DP-C), and predictive health monitoring with telemetry (DP-D). This multi-faceted approach addresses both planned and unplanned downtime through prevention, early detection, and rapid recovery.

potential_sources: Redundancy design (DP-A) must not conflict with modularity constraints (DP-C); requires careful interface standardization.
Health monitoring (DP-D) depends on telemetry infrastructure; must ensure Telemetry interface bandwidth and latency support real-time monitoring.
Predictive maintenance (DP-D) requires integration with maintenance scheduling and spare parts logistics (DP-B); organizational coupling risk.
coupling_check.notes: DP-E is well-coupled to the NGHE mission context. Redundancy and modularity are complementary (both reduce MTTR). Health monitoring enables data-driven maintenance decisions. No fundamental contradictions detected; integration risks are manageable through interface discipline and cross-functional coordination.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Redundant subsystems can be procured and integrated within cost and schedule constraints.
Assumption: Telemetry bandwidth and latency support continuous health monitoring without degrading primary operations.
Risk: Over-redundancy may increase system complexity, weight, and power consumption, creating new failure modes.
Risk: Predictive maintenance algorithms require historical failure data; early production units may lack sufficient training data.
Risk: Modular architecture may introduce additional interface failure points if not carefully designed.
Risk: Maintenance team availability and response time are organizational factors outside system design control.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-E to explicitly specify redundancy strategy (active/active vs. active/passive), modularity standards (mechanical/electrical/software interfaces), and health monitoring scope (which subsystems, which parameters, monitoring frequency). Add cost-benefit analysis to justify redundancy investment against availability target.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: System-level availability orchestration and monitoring. Owns the integrated availability assurance strategy, including redundancy coordination across Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems, health monitoring aggregation, and availability KPI measurement and reporting. Acts as the system integrator for availability.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives health and performance data from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems; used for availability calculation, predictive maintenance, and KPI reporting.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operational tasking from Site Manager/Tele-operator; may pause or redirect tasking based on availability state (e.g., if subsystem degraded).

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Receives safety-critical alerts from Safety Services; safety-induced unavailability is factored into availability calculation.

module_containment_scores.notes: Highest containment. System-level availability orchestration, KPI measurement, and cross-subsystem health monitoring naturally belong at the solution level. Minimal diffusion; only external interfaces are to subsystems and stakeholders.

Low containment. Vehicle owns its own subsystem availability (FR-NGHE-004.1) but cannot own system-level availability orchestration or cross-subsystem redundancy coordination. High diffusion risk if availability logic scattered across Vehicle and other subsystems.

Low containment. Teleoperation owns its subsystem availability (FR-NGHE-004.2) but not system-level orchestration. High diffusion risk; availability logic would be split between Teleoperation and other modules.

Low containment. Safety Services owns safety-induced unavailability (FR-NGHE-004.3) but not overall system availability orchestration. High diffusion risk; availability measurement and reporting would be fragmented.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: NGHE Solution is the natural owner of system-level availability assurance. It has visibility across all subsystems via Telemetry, can coordinate redundancy and failover decisions, and owns the KPI measurement and reporting responsibility. Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services own their respective subsystem availability budgets (sub-FRs 004.1–004.3) but report health data to NGHE Solution for orchestration.
containment_risks: Risk: If NGHE Solution becomes a thin orchestration layer without clear ownership of health monitoring logic, responsibility may diffuse to subsystems, creating inconsistent monitoring and reporting.
Risk: Telemetry interface bandwidth or latency constraints may prevent real-time health monitoring at NGHE Solution level, forcing availability logic to be pushed down to subsystems.
Risk: Organizational silos may prevent NGHE Solution team from having authority to coordinate redundancy decisions across Vehicle and Teleoperation teams.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Explicitly define NGHE Solution's availability orchestration responsibilities: (1) aggregate Telemetry from all subsystems; (2) calculate real-time availability KPI; (3) detect and classify downtime events; (4) trigger predictive maintenance alerts; (5) coordinate failover and degradation decisions. Establish clear interface contracts with Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services for health data reporting. Define escalation paths for availability-critical decisions.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-E (integrated availability assurance) directly addresses FR-NGHE-004 (≥0.97 availability). The combination of redundancy, modularity, and health monitoring is logically sound and well-justified. DP-E is traceable to the refined FR and its sub-FRs. No fundamental mismatch between problem (availability KPI) and solution (multi-faceted assurance strategy).

diffusion_risks: Risk: If health monitoring logic is not centralized in NGHE Solution, it may diffuse across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services, creating inconsistent data collection and reporting.
Risk: Redundancy coordination logic must be owned by NGHE Solution; if pushed to subsystems, availability decisions may be uncoordinated.
Risk: Modularity standards (DP-C) must be enforced across all subsystems; if not, hot-swappable components may not be interchangeable, defeating the purpose.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the appropriate IM boundary for system-level availability orchestration. It has the right scope and visibility. However, success depends on clear interface contracts with subsystems and organizational alignment on availability ownership. Recommend establishing a cross-functional availability working group to ensure DP-E is implemented cohesively.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. Availability is measurable via telemetry and operational logs. KPI calculation is straightforward (uptime / total time). Verification can be automated via continuous monitoring dashboard. Post-evaluation audit against customer-defined target is feasible.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. FR-NGHE-004 is allocated to NGHE Solution (system-level orchestration and KPI measurement). Sub-FRs 004.1–004.3 are allocated to Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services respectively (subsystem availability budgets). FR-NGHE-004.4 and 004.5 (event detection and predictive maintenance) are allocated to NGHE Solution with support from subsystems.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-E to specify redundancy strategy (active/active, active/passive, N+1, etc.) and modularity standards (mechanical, electrical, software interface specifications).
Define Telemetry interface requirements: data types, sampling frequency, latency, bandwidth. Ensure telemetry can support real-time health monitoring without impacting primary operations.

Establish availability measurement methodology: define 'scheduled operating time', 'unplanned downtime', 'planned maintenance windows', and 'safety-induced unavailability'. Align with customer expectations.
Create sub-FR allocation matrix showing which subsystem owns each availability budget and how they contribute to system-level KPI.
Define escalation and decision-making process for availability-critical events (e.g., when to trigger failover, when to degrade service, when to halt operations).

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-004 is a system-level KPI that requires decomposition into subsystem-level availability budgets and operational mechanisms. The refined FR and selected DP are sound, and NGHE Solution is the correct IM boundary. However, the sub-FRs (004.1–004.5) must be further decomposed to define specific design parameters for redundancy, modularity, health monitoring, and maintenance. Additionally, the DP-E strategy requires detailed specification of redundancy architecture, modularity standards, and telemetry requirements before implementation can proceed. Decomposition is necessary to translate the high-level availability assurance strategy into concrete design decisions and module responsibilities.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: Vehicle subsystem shall contribute ≤ 0.015 (1.5%) unplanned downtime to total availability budget.
FR-NGHE-004.2: Teleoperation subsystem shall contribute ≤ 0.010 (1.0%) unplanned downtime to total availability budget.
FR-NGHE-004.3: Safety Services subsystem shall contribute ≤ 0.005 (0.5%) unplanned downtime to total availability budget.
FR-NGHE-004.4: System shall detect and log all downtime events with root cause classification within 5 minutes of occurrence.
FR-NGHE-004.5: System shall provide predictive maintenance recommendations to prevent unplanned failures.

assumptions: Assumption: Customer-defined availability target is ≥0.97 (97%) or will be specified during requirements refinement.
Assumption: Evaluation period is defined (e.g., 12 months of continuous operation).
Assumption: Telemetry infrastructure has sufficient bandwidth and latency to support real-time health monitoring.
Assumption: Redundant subsystems can be procured and integrated within cost and schedule constraints.
Assumption: Maintenance team and spare parts logistics are available to support rapid recovery.
Assumption: Predictive maintenance algorithms can be developed and validated with available historical failure data.
open_risks: Risk: Over-redundancy may increase system complexity, weight, power consumption, and cost, creating new failure modes and reducing ROI.
Risk: Modular architecture may introduce additional interface failure points if not carefully designed and tested.
Risk: Predictive maintenance algorithms require historical failure data; early production units may lack sufficient training data, reducing effectiveness.
Risk: Telemetry interface bandwidth or latency constraints may prevent real-time health monitoring, forcing availability logic to be pushed to subsystems.
Risk: Organizational silos may prevent effective coordination of availability decisions across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services teams.
Risk: Safety-induced unavailability (e.g., emergency stops, safety lockouts) may be difficult to distinguish from failure-induced unavailability, complicating root cause analysis.
evidence_needed: Evidence: Customer-defined availability target and evaluation period (from requirements review).
Evidence: Telemetry interface specifications (bandwidth, latency, data types) from network and communication design.
Evidence: Redundancy architecture trade study (active/active vs. active/passive, cost-benefit analysis).
Evidence: Modularity standards and interface specifications (mechanical, electrical, software) from subsystem design.
Evidence: Predictive maintenance algorithm design and validation plan (data sources, model training, accuracy metrics).
Evidence: Availability measurement methodology and KPI calculation procedure (approved by customer and stakeholders).
Evidence: Maintenance logistics plan (spare parts inventory, response time, team availability).
Evidence: Safety-induced unavailability classification and measurement methodology (coordination with Safety Services).
